steps.text: Paris. A Tuesday like any other — until the mailbox.
Moshé! Leah! Leora said yes! Mumbai! Wedding! Come!
And bring the old brass Hamsa. Sharon insists it belongs with the invitation.
That's five exclamation marks. It must be real.
Mumbai. We'd better start packing tonight.
Neither of them noticed, yet, that the invitation had already begun to change.
At the check-in desk, in full view of both of them, the boarding pass began to erase itself.
The destination block — it's fading. Ink doesn't do that.
And look at the shape of the erased line. That's too short for MUMBAI.
On the counter, where no rabbit could possibly be, sat a rabbit.
Years earlier. A Saturday night in Tel Aviv. Moshé arrived early, rehearsing English sentences that kept collapsing.
(Hello. Shalom. Bonjour. No — commit to one language, coward.)
…Is that a rabbit on the table?
Yes! Rabbit! I mean — yes. It appears we have a rabbit.
It was not a good opening line. It was, however, the beginning of everything.
With the direct flight gone, the route redrew itself: Paris → Istanbul → Muscat → Mumbai.
Three flights instead of one. The long way east.
Someone designed this route. Look — every connection is tight, but never impossible.
Like a puzzle set by someone who knows exactly how fast we work.
Somewhere in Mumbai, Sharon checked a list and smiled.
Arrivals. Garlands, name signs, families colliding into hugs. And for them — no one.
A photograph, face down on the floor. It's the wedding party — but two people are just… blank.
And a brass Hamsa, warm like it's been held all day. With a note: 'Start where you two started.'
Where we started. Moshé — that's not a place in India.
The Hamsa's eye caught the light, and the arrivals hall began, very gently, to dissolve.
Above the lamplit lanes of Mea Shearim, the chapter gathered itself into a glowing fragment.
Israel gave us the rabbit, the missing two in the photos, and a letter drawn by wrong turns.
Every memory here was a door in. Never a door out. Keep that — I think it matters.
Fragment II secured: the rabbit appeared the first time either of them needed an excuse to keep talking.
The wedding was everything the invitation promised — garlands and henna, old melodies in a Mumbai synagogue, two brides laughing through the ceremony.
Six blessings sung. The seventh page… see for yourselves.
The page about building a home. It's not torn out — it was never printed.
Some blessings can't be printed. Some have to be answered.
Sharon. What exactly is your wedding gift to us?
You're standing in it.
Sunset on Koh Phi Phi. The tide coming in. A man holding two coconuts for courage.
Hypothetically. Completely hypothetically. Would you marry me?
Ask me again someday, without the coconuts. Then it counts.
Behind them, unwatched, the evening ferry pulled away from the pier — the ferry to the airport, and the flight home he had once planned to take alone.
On the pier post, exactly where no one looked, the rabbit watched the ferry go.
Eight fragments, eight chapters, one page. The boarding pass lay reassembled under the lamp — a return flight, one seat, dated the morning after a certain sunset.
It was never our flight that was missing. It was yours. The one you never took.
I was supposed to fly home after Thailand. Alone. I'd forgotten I ever planned it.
The reconstruction didn't erase it. You did. The night you asked me a question with a coconut in each hand.
One line of the pass remained blank. Not a destination. A sentence.
The words settled onto the final line, and every anomaly in every memory quietly resolved.
There it is. My whole gift. You crossed nine countries and the only flight that ever mattered is the one that left without you.
The rabbit marked every goodbye we skipped. Your Hamsa kept the true memories safe.
And the photograph? You two were blank because your story wasn't finished being told. Look at it now.
In the photograph, at last, all sixteen faces. The landscapes were never the destination. They were the stage.
